Deep Dive · AI Engineering

Building a Production Hybrid RAG System from Scratch

How to combine vector search and knowledge graphs to build a grounded, hallucination-resistant document Q&A system — with full LLMOps.

🗓️ March 2025 ⏱️ 18 min read 🔗 github.com/VinodPungle/hybrid-rag-capstone
RAG LLMOps Vector Search Knowledge Graph Neo4j FAISS Azure OpenAI FastAPI Streamlit Prometheus Docker Python

📋 In This Article

  1. Why Hybrid RAG? The Case Against Single-Strategy Retrieval
  2. System Architecture — A Bird's-Eye View
  3. The Ingestion Pipeline — From PDF to Searchable Chunks
  4. Vector Search with FAISS — Dense Retrieval
  5. Knowledge Graph with Neo4j — Structured Retrieval
  6. Hybrid Search — Fusing Both Worlds
  7. LLM Answer Generation — Grounded & Faithful
  8. API & UI — Serving the Pipeline
  9. LLMOps — Monitoring, Evaluation & Orchestration
  10. Quickstart — Run It Yourself in 5 Minutes
  11. Key Lessons & What to Build Next

1. Why Hybrid RAG? The Case Against Single-Strategy Retrieval

Standard RAG only uses vector similarity. That's a good start — but it leaves a lot of value on the table.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become the de-facto pattern for building LLM applications over private or specialized documents. The idea is elegant: instead of asking the model to recall facts from its training weights, retrieve the relevant document passages at query time and hand them to the model as verified context.

But the dominant implementation — dense vector retrieval only — has a structural blind spot. Consider a document about corporate governance. A user asks: "Who is responsible for approving the annual audit?"

Vector search will find the paragraph containing the word "audit" with the most similar embedding. It might even find the right paragraph. But it cannot tell you that the CFO chairs the Audit Committee, which reports to the Board, which approves the audit — because that chain of structured relationships isn't captured in any single text chunk.

💡 The Core Insight Vector search is great at "find me text similar to this query."
Graph search is great at "follow these named relationships to find a fact."
Combining both gives the LLM richer, more reliable context for complex questions.

This project — hybrid-rag-capstone — implements exactly this: a production-grade system combining FAISS vector search with a Neo4j knowledge graph, wrapped in a FastAPI REST API, a Streamlit UI, Prometheus/Grafana monitoring, automated evaluation metrics, and Apache Airflow orchestration.

Let's walk through every component, how they fit together, and what the implementation decisions mean in practice.

Python 3.13 Azure OpenAI FAISS Neo4j Aura FastAPI Streamlit Prometheus Grafana Apache Airflow Docker

2. System Architecture — A Bird's-Eye View

The system has two phases: an offline ingestion phase that builds search indexes from a PDF, and an online query phase that retrieves and generates answers.

Hybrid RAG Capstone — Architecture
USER (Browser / Swagger UI / CLI)
         │  PDF Upload     │  Query
         ▼                 ▼
┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────────────┐
│  STREAMLIT   │   │   FASTAPI REST API   │
│  ui/app.py   │   │   api/app.py         │
│  port 8501   │   │   /ingest /ask       │
└──────┬───────┘   └──────────┬───────────┘
       └────────────┬──────────┘
                    ▼
       ┌────────────────────────────┐
       │    INGESTION PIPELINE      │
       │  pdf_loader → chunker      │
       │  → embedder (Azure OAI)    │
       └───────┬────────────────────┘
               │ 1536-dim vectors
       ┌───────┴──────┐   ┌─────────────────────────┐
       │  FAISS INDEX │   │   NEO4J KNOWLEDGE GRAPH  │
       │ faiss_store  │   │  entity_extractor (LLM)  │
       │ IndexFlatL2  │   │  neo4j_store (Cypher)    │
       └───────┬──────┘   └────────────┬────────────┘
               │                       │
               └──────────┬────────────┘
                           ▼  QUERY TIME
              ┌────────────────────────────┐
              │      HYBRID SEARCH         │
              │  search.py (FAISS top-K)   │
              │  graph_search.py (Cypher)  │
              │  → combined_context        │
              └──────────┬─────────────────┘
                         ▼
              ┌────────────────────────────┐
              │   LLM GENERATION           │
              │   generator.py             │
              │   Azure OpenAI GPT-4o-mini │
              └────────────────────────────┘

══════════════ LLMOPS LAYER ══════════════
  config.yaml │ structured logging │ pytest
  Prometheus (9090) │ Grafana (3000)
  Airflow (8080) │ Docker Compose

The pipeline is deliberately modular: each directory is an independent Python package with a clear responsibility. The entire system is driven by config/config.yaml for all tunable parameters, with secrets staying in .env — a clean separation that makes the system reproducible and auditable.

3. The Ingestion Pipeline — From PDF to Searchable Chunks

Ingestion converts a raw PDF into two search indexes: a FAISS vector store and a Neo4j knowledge graph.

3.1 PDF Loading (ingestion/pdf_loader.py)

The load_pdf_text() function uses pypdf to iterate over every page and concatenate the extracted text into a single string. Simple, but the right tool — pypdf handles multi-column layouts, embedded fonts, and standard PDF encodings reliably.

3.2 Text Chunking (ingestion/chunker.py)

A continuous text document can't be searched as-is — it needs to be split into smaller windows that can each be converted to an embedding vector. The chunker uses an overlapping sliding window:

# config.yaml drives these values — no hardcoded magic numbers
chunk_size = 500   # words per chunk
overlap    = 80    # words shared between adjacent chunks
step       = max(chunk_size - overlap, 1)  # defensive guard against infinite loop

while start < len(words):
    chunk = words[start : start + chunk_size]
    chunks.append(' '.join(chunk))
    start += step
🟢 Why overlap matters Without overlap, a sentence spanning a chunk boundary gets split across two chunks — neither contains the complete idea. With 80-word overlap, every boundary region is covered by both adjacent chunks, ensuring retrieval completeness.

The defensive step = max(..., 1) guard prevents an infinite loop if overlap >= chunk_size — a real edge case discovered during unit test development. This is a great example of test-driven bug discovery.

4. Vector Search with FAISS — Dense Retrieval

Converts each text chunk into a 1536-dimensional vector and indexes them for millisecond nearest-neighbor search at query time.

4.1 Embedding Generation (embeddings/embedder.py)

Every chunk is sent to Azure OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large model, which returns a list of 1536 floating-point numbers. The key property: texts with similar meanings get vectors that are geometrically close in 1536-dimensional space. The query at search time goes through the same model, so "audit committee responsibilities" and "What does the audit committee do?" land near each other in vector space — even though the words are different.

resp = client.embeddings.create(
    model = os.getenv('AZURE_OPENAI_EMBEDDING_DEPLOYMENT'),
    input = text
)
vector = resp.data[0].embedding  # list of 1536 floats

4.2 FAISS Index (vector_db/faiss_store.py)

FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is a library for fast nearest-neighbor search over dense vectors. This project uses IndexFlatL2 — exact Euclidean distance computation over all stored vectors.

index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(1536)             # 1536 = embedding dimensions
index.add(np.array(vectors, dtype=np.float32))  # add all chunk vectors

# At query time (search.py):
distances, indices = index.search(query_vec, k=3)  # top-3 similar chunks
💡 When to upgrade from IndexFlatL2 IndexFlatL2 is exact and needs no training, making it perfect for moderate document sizes. For millions of vectors, switch to IndexIVFFlat (clustered approximate search) or a managed vector DB like Pinecone or Azure AI Search.

5. Knowledge Graph with Neo4j — Structured Retrieval

Extracts named entities and relationships from document text using an LLM, stores them in Neo4j, and traverses the graph at query time for structured context.

5.1 Entity Extraction (graph_db/entity_extractor.py)

For each text chunk, the extractor sends a structured JSON extraction prompt to Azure OpenAI GPT-4o-mini. The prompt (loaded from config.yaml) asks the model to return a JSON object with two keys: entities (a list of {name, type} objects) and relationships (a list of {source, target, relation} objects).

# Example LLM output for a governance document chunk:
{
  "entities": [
    {"name": "Audit Committee", "type": "Committee"},
    {"name": "Board of Directors", "type": "Organization"}
  ],
  "relationships": [
    {"source": "Audit Committee",
     "target": "Board of Directors",
     "relation": "reports_to"}
  ]
}

The system supports 8 entity types configured in config.yaml: Person, Organization, Concept, Regulation, Committee, Role, Process, and Document. All extraction parameters (temperature, max_tokens, entity types, prompt template) are config-driven — no hardcoded values in Python.

5.2 Neo4j Storage (graph_db/neo4j_store.py)

Entities are stored as :Entity nodes and relationships as :RELATES_TO directed edges using Cypher's MERGE command — which creates the node/edge only if it doesn't already exist, making ingestion idempotent:

-- Store a relationship (idempotent)
MERGE (a:Entity {name: $source})
MERGE (b:Entity {name: $target})
MERGE (a)-[r:RELATES_TO {relation: $relation}]->(b)
SET r.source_chunk = $source_chunk

5.3 Graph Query (Cypher traversal)

At query time, graph_search.py scans all entity names in Neo4j, finds those whose words match words in the user query, then traverses up to 2 hops outward from each matching entity:

MATCH path = (e:Entity)-[r:RELATES_TO*1..2]->(related)
WHERE toLower(e.name) CONTAINS toLower($name)
RETURN e.name, related.name, [rel IN r | rel.relation] AS rel_chain
LIMIT $limit

This returns strings like "Audit Committee --[oversees]--> Financial Reporting" — structured facts that vector search alone could never surface.

6. Hybrid Search — Fusing Both Worlds

The core of the system: combining dense and structured retrieval into a single context string for the LLM.

The hybrid_search() function in retrieval/hybrid_search.py orchestrates both retrievers and merges their outputs:

def hybrid_search(query, index, chunks, driver=None, top_k=None):
    # 1. Dense retrieval — top-K semantically similar chunks
    vector_results = search(query, index, chunks, top_k=top_k)

    # 2. Graph retrieval — entity relationship traversal
    try:
        graph_results = graph_search(query, driver=driver)
    except Exception as e:
        logger.warning("Graph search failed, falling back: %s", e)
        graph_results = []   # graceful degradation

    # 3. Merge with labeled sections (helps LLM understand provenance)
    context_parts = [
        "=== Document Context ===", *vector_results,
        "\n=== Knowledge Graph Context ===", *graph_results
    ]
    combined_context = "\n".join(context_parts)

    return {"vector_results": vector_results,
            "graph_results": graph_results,
            "combined_context": combined_context}
🟢 Graceful Degradation by Design The graph search failure is logged as a warning, not an exception. If Neo4j is unavailable (network issue, free tier limit, misconfigured credentials), the API continues serving requests using vector-only results. This is a production-critical design decision — partial functionality beats complete downtime.

The labeled sections (=== Document Context === and === Knowledge Graph Context ===) are a deliberate prompt engineering choice. They signal to the LLM the nature of each context type — allowing it to draw on dense passages for narrative answers and graph relationship strings for structured facts.

7. LLM Answer Generation — Grounded & Faithful

The generator assembles the final prompt and calls Azure OpenAI, with built-in hallucination guards.

The generate_answer() function in llm/generator.py builds the prompt from templates in config.yaml and calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o-mini:

# System prompt (from config.yaml):
"You are a helpful document understanding assistant.
Answer questions based only on the provided context."

# User prompt template:
"""Answer ONLY using the context below.
If the answer cannot be found, say 'I cannot answer this based on the provided context.'

Context:
{combined_context}

Question: {query}
Answer:"""

The explicit constraint "Answer ONLY using the context below" combined with a clear fallback instruction is the primary anti-hallucination mechanism. When this is enforced, the LLM cannot drift into its training data to fill gaps — it either answers from context or admits it cannot.

Every LLM call records token usage and wall-clock latency, both as structured logs (via utils/logger.py) and as Prometheus metrics (via monitoring/prometheus_metrics.py). This is standard LLMOps practice for cost control and performance monitoring.

8. API & UI — Serving the Pipeline

8.1 FastAPI REST API (api/app.py)

The API exposes three core endpoints. All request and response shapes are defined as Pydantic models, giving automatic validation, serialization, and Swagger UI documentation for free:

MethodEndpointWhat It Does
GET/healthReturns service status and whether a document is loaded
POST/ingestUploads a PDF, runs full ingestion, returns chunk/entity/relationship counts + elapsed time
POST/askSubmits a query JSON, returns answer + vector results + graph results + latency
GET/metricsPrometheus scrape endpoint (auto-exposed by prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator)

The API uses FastAPI's lifespan handler to cleanly close the Neo4j driver on shutdown — a common resource leak in demo projects that's properly handled here.

8.2 Streamlit Web UI (ui/app.py)

The Streamlit interface provides a complete demo experience: PDF upload in the sidebar, document loading with progress spinners, a question input with validation, a side-by-side display of vector and graph results, and an interactive knowledge graph visualization rendered with streamlit-agraph. All UI configuration (page title, entity colors, graph dimensions) is driven by config.yaml — a clean separation between code and presentation.

9. LLMOps — Monitoring, Evaluation & Orchestration

What separates a demo from a production system: observability, quality measurement, and automation.

9.1 Configuration Management

All tunable parameters live in config/config.yaml. Secrets stay in .env. Python code accesses config via cfg('section', 'key') from config/settings.py. This follows the Twelve-Factor App principle — separating config from code makes the system deployable across environments without modification.

9.2 Structured Logging (utils/logger.py)

All print() statements have been replaced with a structured logger. Every module calls get_logger(__name__) and logs at appropriate levels (info, warning, error, debug). This enables log aggregation, filtering, and alerting in production — you can't search or alert on print() output.

9.3 Prometheus Metrics (monitoring/prometheus_metrics.py)

Six custom metrics track the health and performance of the pipeline in real time:

MetricTypeMeasures
rag_requests_totalCounterTotal /ingest and /ask requests, by endpoint
rag_request_latency_secondsHistogramEnd-to-end request latency (p50, p95, p99)
llm_tokens_totalCounterPrompt + completion tokens consumed per call type
llm_call_latency_secondsHistogramIndividual Azure OpenAI API call duration
graph_entities_currentGaugeCurrent entity count in the knowledge graph
vector_results_countHistogramNumber of vector search results per query

9.4 Evaluation Metrics (evaluation/metrics.py)

Three standard NLP metrics measure answer quality automatically, controlled by config.yaml:

  • BLEU-1 to 4: N-gram precision between generated and reference answers
  • ROUGE-1/2/L: N-gram recall — how much of the reference is captured
  • Faithfulness: Custom metric scoring what fraction of answer sentences are grounded in the source context — the most important metric for hallucination detection in RAG

9.5 Docker & Orchestration

The full stack runs with a single command. Docker Compose defines six services with health checks and dependency ordering:

docker compose --profile monitoring up --build

# Services started:
#  API        → http://localhost:8000/docs
#  UI         → http://localhost:8501
#  Prometheus → http://localhost:9090
#  Grafana    → http://localhost:3000 (admin/admin)

Apache Airflow (available via --profile airflow) orchestrates the pipeline as a DAG — enabling scheduled re-ingestion, automated evaluation runs, and pipeline monitoring through the Airflow UI.

10. Quickstart — Run It Yourself in 5 Minutes

You need Python 3.13, an Azure OpenAI account with two deployments, and a Neo4j Aura Free account.

Clone Repo
→
pip install
→
Create .env
→
streamlit run
→
Upload PDF
→
Ask Away
# 1. Clone
git clone https://github.com/VinodPungle/hybrid-rag-capstone.git
cd hybrid-rag-capstone

# 2. Install dependencies
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate  # or .venv\Scripts\activate on Windows
pip install -r requirements.txt

# 3. Create .env with your credentials
AZURE_OPENAI_KEY=...
AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT=https://your-resource.openai.azure.com/
AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT=gpt-4o-mini
AZURE_OPENAI_EMBEDDING_KEY=...
AZURE_OPENAI_EMBEDDING_ENDPOINT=https://your-resource.openai.azure.com/
AZURE_OPENAI_EMBEDDING_DEPLOYMENT=text-embedding-3-large
NEO4J_URI=neo4j+ssc://your-id.databases.neo4j.io
NEO4J_USER=neo4j
NEO4J_PASSWORD=...

# 4. Start the Streamlit UI
streamlit run ui/app.py
# → Open http://localhost:8501

# Or the REST API:
uvicorn api.app:app --reload
# → Open http://localhost:8000/docs
🟢 Recommended Test Documents For the best demo, try a multi-page governance document (annual report, SEC filing, policy document) — something with named organizations, roles, committees, and regulations. The knowledge graph extraction shines on structured organizational text.

11. Key Lessons & What to Build Next

What this project teaches beyond the code — and where to take it further.

Lessons from the Codebase

⚙️

Config-Driven Code

Every tunable value — chunk size, temperature, prompts — is in config.yaml. Change behavior without touching Python.

🛡️

Graceful Degradation

Graph failures fall back to vector-only. The system never fails completely — it just delivers less-enriched results.

📊

Measure Everything

Prometheus metrics on every LLM call. Faithfulness scoring on every answer. You can't improve what you don't measure.

🔒

Secrets Hygiene

API keys in .env, never in source. Config params in config.yaml, committed to git. Clean separation.

🧪

Test While Building

The step = max(..., 1) defensive guard in chunker.py was discovered during pytest development. Tests catch real bugs.

🐳

Containerise Early

Docker Compose from day one means every collaborator runs the same stack. No "works on my machine" surprises.

Suggested Next Steps

Ready to extend this project? Here are the highest-impact improvements, in order of complexity:

  1. Persistent vector store — Replace the in-memory FAISS index with a persistent vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, or Azure AI Search) so the index survives server restarts and scales to thousands of documents.
  2. Multi-document support — Add document metadata (title, date, source) to FAISS and Neo4j so users can filter by document and the system can cite its sources in responses.
  3. Reranking — Add a cross-encoder reranker (e.g., cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2) after hybrid retrieval to improve result quality before the LLM call.
  4. Streaming responses — Add Server-Sent Events to the FastAPI /ask endpoint so the Streamlit UI renders the answer token-by-token, improving perceived latency.
  5. RAG evaluation with LLM judges — Replace BLEU/ROUGE with LLM-as-judge evaluation (using frameworks like RAGAS) for more nuanced quality assessment.
⚠️ Production Consideration IndexFlatL2 scales to ~100K vectors comfortably. Beyond that, switch to an approximate index (IndexIVFFlat, IndexHNSWFlat) or a managed vector DB. The architecture here is designed to make this swap straightforward — only faiss_store.py and retrieval/search.py need to change.

Conclusion

The hybrid-rag-capstone is more than a RAG demo — it's a template for how to build production AI systems with Python. Every component has a clear responsibility, every parameter is configurable, every LLM call is measured, and the whole stack runs with a single command.

The core insight — that combining dense vector retrieval with structured graph retrieval gives the LLM fundamentally better context — generalises well beyond governance documents to any domain with named entities and structured relationships: legal, medical, financial, technical.

Clone it, break it, extend it. The best way to learn this stack is to load a document you care about and start asking questions.

📎 Resources GitHub Repository · Neo4j Graph Academy (Free) · FAISS Documentation · FastAPI Docs · Prometheus Introduction · RAGAS Evaluation Framework
Based on github.com/VinodPungle/hybrid-rag-capstone  ·  Written March 2025  ·  Stack: Python · Azure OpenAI · FAISS · Neo4j · FastAPI · Streamlit · Docker
